items.question: Which approach best describes NumPy vectorization?
options: Calling a Python function on each array element inside a for-loop
Applying whole-array operations that run in compiled NumPy kernels
Converting arrays to Python lists for faster iteration
Using np.vectorize to compile arbitrary Python lambdas to C
items.answer: Applying whole-array operations that run in compiled NumPy kernels
items.explanation: Vectorization delegates numeric work to ufuncs and reductions implemented in compiled code. Python per-element loops and np.vectorize still pay interpreter cost per element.

items.question: Given X with shape (1000, 12) and mean vector mu with shape (12,), what is the shape of X - mu?
items.explanation: mu broadcasts across the row dimension: trailing dimension 12 matches, and the missing row axis in mu is treated as size 1 stretched across 1000 rows.

items.question: When will NumPy raise ValueError for broadcasting?
options: When one array has shape (n, 1) and the other has shape (1, m)
When one array has shape (n, m) and the other has shape (m,)
When one array has shape (n, m) and the other has shape (n,)
When both arrays are contiguous float64
items.answer: When one array has shape (n, m) and the other has shape (n,)
items.explanation: For (n, m) vs (n,), trailing dims m vs n differ and neither is 1, so broadcasting fails. (n,1) with (1,m) broadcasts to (n,m); (n,m) with (m,) broadcasts by aligning m.

items.question: What is the main performance problem with nested Python loops over large NumPy arrays?
options: NumPy cannot store arrays larger than loops allow
Interpreter overhead per element dominates over the numeric work
Loops automatically convert arrays to object dtype
items.answer: Interpreter overhead per element dominates over the numeric work
items.explanation: Each loop iteration runs through Python dispatch. On hundreds of thousands of elements, that overhead far exceeds the cost of the arithmetic itself.

items.question: Which replacement is correct for squaring every element of array x without a loop?
options: for i in range(len(x)): x[i] = x[i] ** 2
items.explanation: The ** operator invokes a true vectorized ufunc on the ndarray. list comprehensions and explicit loops stay in Python; np.vectorize still calls Python per element.
